state.name: New Hampshire
state.agency: New Hampshire Fish and Game Department
state.peak_intrusion_season: March through May for spring denning, late summer for bear pressure, and October through November as animals seek winter shelter
state.climate_factor: New Hampshire's long, harsh winters drive rodents, bats, and squirrels into attics aggressively, and the state's growing bear population makes spring and fall the busiest seasons for residential conflicts
state.permit_note: New Hampshire requires a Wildlife Control Operator license for commercial trappers, and bears, bobcats, and migratory birds carry additional state and federal handling restrictions

Rockingham County.region: southeastern New Hampshire along the Atlantic coast and Massachusetts border
Rockingham County.nearest_metro: Boston, MA (about 50 miles south of Portsmouth)
Rockingham County.habitat_type: the most developed county in the state, with dense suburban towns, the small Atlantic coastline, and the Great Bay tidal estuary
Rockingham County.regional_wildlife: coyotes are well-established in every suburban town here, and bat colonies in older Portsmouth and Exeter homes are a recurring summer issue
Rockingham County.absent_or_rare: moose are rare in this developed southern coastal area

Hillsborough County.region: south-central New Hampshire along the Massachusetts border, the most populous county in the state
Hillsborough County.nearest_metro: Boston, MA (about 50 miles south of Nashua)
Hillsborough County.habitat_type: the urban Manchester and Nashua cores, surrounding suburban towns, and significant wooded areas in the western part of the county
Hillsborough County.regional_wildlife: fisher cats are widely reported in suburban yards across the county, and black bear sightings in residential neighborhoods have steadily increased
Hillsborough County.absent_or_rare: moose occur in the wooded northern and western edges but are uncommon in the populated south

Merrimack County.region: central New Hampshire along the Merrimack River, including the state capital
Merrimack County.habitat_type: the small urban core of Concord, river-valley suburbs, and substantial forest in the surrounding hills
Merrimack County.regional_wildlife: bear activity around Concord-area residential neighborhoods is a recurring spring and fall issue, and beavers cause flooding problems along several smaller tributaries
Merrimack County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but more concentrated to the north

Strafford County.region: southeastern New Hampshire between the seacoast and the lakes region
Strafford County.habitat_type: small cities, university-area neighborhoods around Durham, and rural towns transitioning toward the lakes region
Strafford County.regional_wildlife: the UNH area in Durham generates heavy raccoon and skunk pressure around student housing, and bear activity has expanded into Rochester and Barrington
Strafford County.absent_or_rare: moose sightings are uncommon in this corner of the state

Cheshire County.region: southwestern New Hampshire along the Connecticut River and the Massachusetts and Vermont borders
Cheshire County.nearest_metro: Brattleboro, VT (about 20 miles west) and Manchester (about 60 miles east)
Cheshire County.habitat_type: wooded hills, small towns and farmland around the Monadnock region, and the Connecticut River valley
Cheshire County.regional_wildlife: the Monadnock region has heavy bear pressure on residential properties, and porcupines are an established nuisance for outbuildings and decks
Cheshire County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but mostly in the higher, more remote sections of the county

Grafton County.region: western New Hampshire along the Connecticut River, including much of the White Mountains
Grafton County.nearest_metro: the Lebanon-Hanover area is its own regional hub; Manchester is about 70 miles southeast
Grafton County.habitat_type: the steep terrain of the White Mountains, the Upper Connecticut River valley, and the Dartmouth College area
Grafton County.regional_wildlife: Grafton County has some of the highest moose density in the state, and bear conflicts at vacation homes throughout the White Mountains region are routine
Grafton County.absent_or_rare: open-country species like coyotes are present but less concentrated than in southern counties

Belknap County.region: central New Hampshire's lakes region, centered on Lake Winnipesaukee
Belknap County.habitat_type: lakeshore communities, vacation-home developments, and surrounding wooded hills
Belknap County.regional_wildlife: lakeshore properties around Winnipesaukee have heavy bat, raccoon, and bear pressure, and seasonal vacation homes routinely host overwintering rodents
Belknap County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but less concentrated than in Grafton or Coos counties

Carroll County.region: east-central New Hampshire including the eastern White Mountains and the Maine border
Carroll County.nearest_metro: Portland, ME (about 60 miles east) and Concord (about 70 miles south)
Carroll County.habitat_type: mountain valleys, lake communities, and large blocks of national forest
Carroll County.regional_wildlife: the Conway and North Conway area has constant bear conflict at vacation properties and dumpsters, and moose-related calls are common in the more remote valleys
Carroll County.absent_or_rare: open-country species are scarce given the heavy forest cover

Sullivan County.region: western New Hampshire along the Connecticut River south of Grafton County
Sullivan County.nearest_metro: Lebanon (about 20 miles north) and Concord (about 50 miles east)
Sullivan County.habitat_type: small mill towns along the Connecticut River and rural wooded hills inland, including the Sunapee region
Sullivan County.regional_wildlife: Sunapee-area lake homes and the rural towns east of Claremont see frequent bear and raccoon issues at residential properties
Sullivan County.absent_or_rare: moose are present but at lower densities than the counties to the north

Coos County.region: the entire northern tip of New Hampshire, bordering Quebec, Maine, and Vermont — the most remote county in the state
Coos County.nearest_metro: the region is its own remote hub; Portland, ME and Concord are each about 100+ miles away
Coos County.habitat_type: the most heavily forested county in the state, with mountains, vast working timberlands, and a small number of mill towns
Coos County.regional_wildlife: Coos County has the highest moose density in New Hampshire, and bear, fisher, and porcupine encounters at residential and camp properties are routine year-round
Coos County.absent_or_rare: the region's harsh climate and remote character make commercial wildlife services more limited than in southern New Hampshire
